claim.content: The combination of experimental facts — (i) the presence of a strongly anisotropic and persistent ESR line in YbRh2Si2, (ii) a g-factor whose temperature and magnetic-field dependences mirror the temperature- and field-dependent Sommerfeld coefficient γ = C_el/T and the 29Si NMR Knight shift and 1/(T1T), and (iii) a linewidth that acquires a T² low-temperature behaviour together with a field-dependent residual broadening — demonstrates that the observed ESR in YbRh2Si2 is a resonance excitation of the coherent heavy quasiparticles that form below the single-ion Kondo (spin-fluctuation) scale T_K ≃ T_0 ≈ 25 K. Consequently, the ESR parameters (the resonance field B_res or equivalently the resonance g, and the linewidth ΔB) are governed by the field- and temperature-dependent quasiparticle effective mass m*(B,T), the quasiparticle density of states at the Fermi level N(E_F;B,T), and the quasiparticle spin relaxation times; therefore ESR provides direct experimental access to the dynamics and evolution of the Kondo state and to the crossover between non–Fermi-liquid and Landau Fermi-liquid regimes in this Kondo-lattice system.

conclusion.content: The combination of experimental facts — (i) the presence of a strongly anisotropic and persistent ESR line in YbRh2Si2, (ii) a g-factor whose temperature and magnetic-field dependences mirror the temperature- and field-dependent Sommerfeld coefficient γ = C_el/T and the 29Si NMR Knight shift and 1/(T1T), and (iii) a linewidth that acquires a T² low-temperature behaviour together with a field-dependent residual broadening — demonstrates that the observed ESR in YbRh2Si2 is a resonance excitation of the coherent heavy quasiparticles that form below the single-ion Kondo (spin-fluctuation) scale T_K ≃ T_0 ≈ 25 K. Consequently, the ESR parameters (the resonance field B_res or equivalently the resonance g, and the linewidth ΔB) are governed by the field- and temperature-dependent quasiparticle effective mass m*(B,T), the quasiparticle density of states at the Fermi level N(E_F;B,T), and the quasiparticle spin relaxation times; therefore ESR provides direct experimental access to the dynamics and evolution of the Kondo state and to the crossover between non–Fermi-liquid and Landau Fermi-liquid regimes in this Kondo-lattice system.

premises.content:
The identification of the observed ESR in YbRh2Si2 as a collective resonance of the coherent heavy quasiparticles formed below T_0 ≈ 25 K rests on (a) the empirical coincidence of crossovers in the ESR g-factor and linewidth with crossovers seen in thermodynamic (Sommerfeld coefficient γ) and 29Si NMR observables, and (b) theoretical proposals that many-body narrowing, aided by ferromagnetic correlations or by anisotropic electron-electron interactions, can render a collective ESR observable in a Kondo lattice; this identification therefore requires that (i) a collective heavy-quasiparticle mode exists with a sufficiently long lifetime and that the mode inherits the anisotropic g-factor character of the 4f states through strong hybridization, and (ii) quantitatively adequate alternative scenarios — such as a residual ESR signal of localized Yb3+ moments surviving despite Kondo screening, a conventional conduction-electron spin resonance surviving in spite of strong spin-orbit scattering, or an electron-bottleneck cross-relaxation regime between localized and itinerant spins — are excluded in the same temperature–field domain.
      Theoretical proposals cited in the paper: Abrahams & Wölfle (arXiv:0808.0892), Zvyagin et al. (unpublished)

steps.reasoning: State which upstream conclusions are being taken as established starting points for this derivation: accept as known the results of conclusion 1 (a clear, strongly anisotropic ESR resonance exists for $B\perp c$ and persists up to $\sim 8\,$T), conclusion 2 (the ESR $g$-factor $g(T,B)$ shows a $-\ln T$ to constant crossover and its low-$T$ change correlates with the Sommerfeld coefficient $\gamma$ and $^{29}$Si NMR quantities, so $g$ reflects the heavy-quasiparticle spin susceptibility), and conclusion 3 (the ESR linewidth $\Delta B(T)$ exhibits a hump near $7$–$8\,$K and a low-$T$ $\Delta B\propto T^{2}$ regime in strong fields with residual width scaling $\sim B^{2}$, consistent with a change to QP–QP scattering).

Summarize how the anisotropy observed in conclusion 1 constrains the microscopic character of the resonant species: the observed strong anisotropy (a large $g$ for $B\perp c$ and no detectable ESR for $B\parallel c$) mirrors the strong single-ion-like magnetic anisotropy of Yb$^{3+}$ 4f states and thus indicates that the resonant degrees of freedom inherit substantial 4f-anisotropic character rather than being featureless, isotropic conduction-electron spin resonance; this observation is compatible with quasiparticles that are coherent hybrids of 4f and conduction-electron states and thereby carry anisotropic $g$-tensor fingerprints of the 4f orbitals. 
Fig. 1

Use conclusion 2 to link the ESR static response to quasiparticle thermodynamics: because the ESR $g$-factor exhibits the same field- and temperature-dependent enhancement and crossover as the Sommerfeld coefficient $\gamma$ and $^{29}$Si NMR Knight shift (all quantities sensitive to $N(E_{F})$ and $m^{*}$), the uniform transverse spin response probed by ESR is tracking the heavy-quasiparticle spin susceptibility $\chi_{p}\propto N(E_{F})\propto m^{*}$; this implies that the resonance frequency (encoded in $g$) is governed by the field-dependent quasiparticle effective mass $m^{*}(B,T)$ and density of states $N(E_{F};B,T)$ rather than by purely local unscreened 4f moments or ordinary conduction electrons. 
Fig. 2

Use conclusion 3 to connect the ESR relaxation dynamics to quasiparticle scattering: the emergence of a low-temperature $\Delta B\propto T^{2}$ linewidth law and a residual width scaling approximately as $B^{2}$ indicates that spin relaxation of the resonant species is governed at low $T$ and in strong fields by quasiparticle–quasiparticle scattering channels characteristic of a Landau Fermi-liquid; since such QP–QP channels are the expected dominant spin-relaxation mechanisms for coherent heavy quasiparticles, the linewidth behavior supports identifying the resonant entity as the heavy quasiparticles. 
Fig. 3

Integrate the static (g-factor) and dynamic (linewidth) evidence together with the anisotropy to reach the identification: combining (i) the anisotropic and persistent ESR line that carries 4f-like anisotropy, (ii) the $g$-factor temperature and field dependences that quantitatively mirror thermodynamic and NMR indicators of heavy-quasiparticle formation (Sommerfeld coefficient $\gamma$ and $^{29}$Si NMR Knight shift and $1/(T_{1}T)$), and (iii) the linewidth evolution into a $T^{2}$ low-temperature law with $B^{2}$-like residual broadening consistent with QP–QP scattering in an LFL, one concludes that the observed ESR is a resonance excitation of the heavy quasiparticles formed below the single-ion Kondo scale $T_{K}\sim T_{0}\approx 25\,$K rather than a simple localized-ion or pure conduction-electron resonance. 
Fig. 1
Fig. 2

State the consequence of this identification for the interpretation and use of ESR in this material: as a consequence of identifying the resonance as a heavy-electron spin resonance, ESR parameters (resonance field/resonance $g$, linewidth $\Delta B$) are governed by the field- and temperature-dependent quasiparticle effective mass $m^{*}(B,T)$, density of states $N(E_{F};B,T)$, and their spin relaxation times $T_{1}$/$T_{2}^{*}$; therefore ESR provides direct experimental access to the dynamics and evolution of the Kondo state and the crossover between non-Fermi-liquid (NFL) and LFL regimes in this Kondo lattice. 
Fig. 3

Record the authors' qualification about the level of theoretical completeness: the identification is presented as a qualitative/conjectural interpretation grounded in the ensemble of experimental correspondences rather than as a full microscopic derivation; the paper explicitly recognizes that the precise microscopic mechanism by which coherent Kondo hybridization produces a narrow, anisotropic collective ESR mode and the quantitative inheritance of 4f anisotropy by itinerant quasiparticles require further theoretical development. 
[14]

motivating_questions.content: A central unresolved issue was whether ESR could serve as a direct probe of heavy-quasiparticle dynamics in a concentrated Kondo lattice: previous narrow-ESR observations were ambiguous between local-moment and conduction-electron scenarios and hence could not be used to study quasiparticle formation and crossovers experimentally. There existed no comprehensive experimental demonstration that ESR observables quantitatively follow the same field- and temperature-dependent renormalizations known from thermodynamics and NMR for heavy fermions. Without establishing ESR as a heavy-quasiparticle resonance, it was impossible to exploit ESR to follow the evolution of the Kondo state with applied field and temperature. Demonstrating that ESR reflects $m^{*}(B,T)$ and QP lifetimes fills this gap and enables a new spectroscopic window onto Kondo-lattice many-body physics.

paper:867762218521854503.authors: U. Schaufuß | V. Kataev | A.A. Zvyagin | B. Büchner | J. Sichelschmidt | J. Wykhoff | C. Krellner | C. Geibel | F. Steglich
paper:867762218521854503.en_title: Evolution of the Kondo state of YbRh2Si2 probed by high field ESR
paper:867762218521854503.zh_title: 通过高场电子自旋共振探测YbRh₂Si₂近藤态的演化
